quiz_questions.question_text: Two categorical variables are said to be associated if:

options.text: They are measured in the same units
The distribution of one variable changes depending on the value of the other variable
Both variables are numerical
They have the same number of categories

quiz_questions.explanation: Two categorical variables are associated when knowing the value of one variable tells you something about the distribution of the other. The conditional distributions differ across groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a two-way table, 70% of males and 70% of females prefer pizza. Are gender and pizza preference associated?

options.text: Yes, because 70% is high
No, because the conditional distributions are the same for both genders
Yes, because both groups prefer pizza
Cannot be determined

quiz_questions.explanation: If the conditional relative frequencies are identical across groups (70% for both males and females), the variables are independent. There is no association between gender and pizza preference.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study finds that 80% of athletes eat breakfast, while only 45% of non-athletes eat breakfast. What can be concluded?

options.text: There is no association between athletic status and breakfast habits
There appears to be an association between athletic status and breakfast habits
Being an athlete causes people to eat breakfast
Eating breakfast causes people to become athletes

quiz_questions.explanation: The large difference in conditional frequencies (80% vs. 45%) indicates an association between athletic status and breakfast habits. However, association does not prove causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two categorical variables are independent, what must be true about their conditional relative frequencies?

options.text: They must all equal zero
They must be approximately equal across all categories of the other variable
They must sum to 2
They must all be different

quiz_questions.explanation: If two variables are independent, the conditional distribution of one variable is the same regardless of the category of the other variable. The conditional relative frequencies should be approximately equal across groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following provides the best evidence of an association between two categorical variables?

options.text: Equal row totals in a two-way table
Large differences in conditional relative frequencies across categories
A large grand total
Equal joint frequencies in all cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Association is identified by comparing conditional relative frequencies. If these proportions differ substantially across categories, there is evidence of an association between the variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: A survey shows that 40% of children, 60% of teenagers, and 55% of adults prefer chocolate ice cream. Is there an association between age group and ice cream preference?

options.text: No, because chocolate is popular in all groups
Yes, because the conditional frequencies vary across age groups (40%, 60%, 55%)
No, because all percentages are above 30%
Cannot determine without the grand total

quiz_questions.explanation: Because the conditional relative frequencies differ across age groups (40%, 60%, 55%), there appears to be an association between age group and ice cream preference.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which method is used to determine if there is an association between two categorical variables?

options.text: Calculating the correlation coefficient
Comparing conditional relative frequencies across groups in a two-way table
Drawing a scatter plot
Calculating the mean of both variables

quiz_questions.explanation: Association between categorical variables is determined by comparing conditional relative frequencies. If these proportions differ significantly across groups, the variables are associated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A two-way table shows that 30% of dog owners and 28% of cat owners exercise daily. Is there strong evidence of association?

options.text: Yes, because the percentages are different
No, because the difference (2 percentage points) is very small, suggesting little to no association
Yes, because both are below 50%
Cannot determine without more data

quiz_questions.explanation: A difference of only 2 percentage points (30% vs. 28%) is very small and does not suggest a meaningful association. Small differences in conditional frequencies often occur by chance.

quiz_questions.question_text: If there is an association between two variables, does this mean one causes the other?

options.text: Yes, association always means causation
No, association only means the variables are related, not that one causes the other
Yes, but only if the association is strong
No, unless the grand total exceeds 1000

quiz_questions.explanation: Association between categorical variables indicates a relationship or pattern, but it does not prove causation. Confounding variables or coincidence could explain the association.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two categorical variables appear independent. Which of the following must be true?

options.text: All joint frequencies are equal
The conditional distribution of one variable is the same across all categories of the other
The marginal frequencies are equal
The grand total equals 100

quiz_questions.explanation: When two categorical variables are independent, the conditional distribution of one variable does not change based on the category of the other variable. The proportions remain the same across groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is association in the context of categorical data?

options.text: A numerical measure of the slope between two variables
A relationship between two categorical variables where the distribution of one variable differs depending on the category of the other
When two variables have the same number of categories
When two variables are measured at the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: Association between categorical variables exists when the distribution (or conditional relative frequencies) of one variable changes depending on which category of the other variable you examine.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for two categorical variables to be independent?

options.text: They are measured using different instruments
Knowing the value of one variable provides no information about the distribution of the other variable
They have the same marginal frequencies
All cells in the two-way table are empty

quiz_questions.explanation: Two categorical variables are independent when knowing the category of one variable does not help predict or change the distribution of the other variable. Their conditional distributions are identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a conditional distribution?

options.text: The overall distribution of a single variable
The distribution of one variable for a specific category of another variable
The joint frequency divided by itself
A distribution that only includes outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: A conditional distribution shows how one variable is distributed within a particular category of another variable. For example, the distribution of ice cream preferences among females only.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a segmented bar graph?

options.text: A bar graph where each bar is divided into segments representing the proportions of a second categorical variable
A bar graph with gaps between all bars
A scatter plot with bars instead of dots
A histogram of numerical data

quiz_questions.explanation: A segmented bar graph (or stacked bar graph) divides each bar into segments that represent the proportions of a second categorical variable. It is useful for visually comparing conditional distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a mosaic plot display?

options.text: The relationship between two numerical variables
The joint and conditional distributions of two categorical variables using rectangles whose areas represent frequencies
The distribution of a single variable
The residuals from a regression line

quiz_questions.explanation: A mosaic plot uses rectangles whose widths represent the marginal distribution of one variable and whose heights represent the conditional distribution of the other, with areas proportional to cell frequencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'no association' between two categorical variables look like in a segmented bar graph?

options.text: Bars of very different heights
Segments within each bar are approximately the same proportions across all bars
Only one color appears in each bar
The bars disappear

quiz_questions.explanation: When there is no association, the conditional distributions are the same for each category, so the segments within each bar have approximately the same proportions regardless of which bar you look at.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between association and independence in categorical data?

options.text: Association means the variables are related; independence means they are not related
Association means causation; independence means correlation
They mean the same thing
Association applies to numerical data; independence applies to categorical data

quiz_questions.explanation: Association means that the conditional distributions of one variable differ across categories of the other, indicating a relationship. Independence means the conditional distributions are the same, indicating no relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of sample size when determining association?

options.text: Sample size is irrelevant
Larger sample sizes provide more reliable evidence of association, while small differences in small samples may be due to chance
Only samples of exactly 100 can show association
Larger samples always show stronger associations

quiz_questions.explanation: With small sample sizes, observed differences in conditional frequencies may be due to random chance. Larger samples provide more reliable evidence, making it easier to distinguish a real association from random variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a side-by-side bar graph show in the context of categorical association?

options.text: The joint frequencies for each combination, displayed as adjacent bars for easy comparison of distributions
Only the marginal frequencies
The mean of each category
The correlation coefficient

quiz_questions.explanation: A side-by-side bar graph places bars for different categories next to each other, making it easy to visually compare the distributions and identify potential associations between the categorical variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'practical significance' mean when evaluating association?

options.text: Whether the association is large enough to matter in a real-world context, not just statistically detectable
Whether the p-value is less than 0.05
Whether the sample was collected correctly
Whether the data is categorical

quiz_questions.explanation: Practical significance considers whether an observed association is large enough to be meaningful in a real-world context. A small difference might be statistically detectable with large samples but not important enough to act upon.

quiz_questions.question_text: A phone manufacturer surveys 600 customers about phone brand (Brand A, Brand B) and satisfaction (Satisfied, Neutral, Dissatisfied). Brand A: Satisfied=180, Neutral=60, Dissatisfied=60. Brand B: Satisfied=120, Neutral=90, Dissatisfied=90. Is there an association between brand and satisfaction?

options.text: No, because both brands have customers in all satisfaction levels
Yes, because Brand A has 60% satisfaction while Brand B has only 40% satisfaction
No, because both brands have equal totals
Yes, because Brand B has more neutral responses

quiz_questions.explanation: Brand A: 180/300 = 60% satisfied. Brand B: 120/300 = 40% satisfied. The conditional distributions differ substantially (60% vs 40% satisfied), indicating an association between brand and satisfaction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health clinic surveys 500 patients about exercise frequency (Regular, Occasional, None) and flu occurrence last year (Yes, No). Regular: Yes=15, No=185. Occasional: Yes=30, No=120. None: Yes=45, No=105. Is there evidence of an association between exercise and flu?

options.text: No, because some people who exercise still got the flu
Yes, because the flu rate increases as exercise decreases: 7.5% for regular, 20% for occasional, 30% for none
No, because more people did not get the flu in all groups
Yes, but only for the 'None' group

quiz_questions.explanation: Regular: 15/200 = 7.5% flu. Occasional: 30/150 = 20% flu. None: 45/150 = 30% flu. The conditional flu rates differ substantially across exercise groups, showing a clear association.

quiz_questions.question_text: A college admissions office tracks acceptance by gender (Male, Female) and major (STEM, Non-STEM). Overall, 40% of males and 30% of females are accepted. However, when broken down by major, 50% of males in STEM and 55% of females in STEM are accepted, and 20% of males in Non-STEM and 22% of females in Non-STEM are accepted. What phenomenon does this illustrate?

options.text: Correlation equals causation
Simpson's Paradox, where the overall trend reverses when data is separated into subgroups
The data must contain errors
Gender has no effect on acceptance

quiz_questions.explanation: This is Simpson's Paradox. Overall, males have a higher acceptance rate, but within each major, females actually have slightly higher rates. This occurs because more males apply to the higher-acceptance STEM major, skewing the overall numbers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marketing agency surveys 800 consumers about shopping channel (Online, In-Store) and age group (18-35, 36-55, 56+). 18-35: Online=180, In-Store=70. 36-55: Online=150, In-Store=150. 56+: Online=50, In-Store=200. Create a segmented bar chart mentally and determine which age group has the strongest preference for in-store shopping.

options.text: All groups prefer in-store equally

quiz_questions.explanation: 18-35: In-Store = 70/250 = 28%. 36-55: In-Store = 150/300 = 50%. 56+: In-Store = 200/250 = 80%. The 56+ age group has the strongest preference for in-store shopping at 80%.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school surveys 400 students about after-school activity (Sports, Clubs, None) and academic performance (Above Average, Average, Below Average). Sports: Above=60, Avg=40, Below=20. Clubs: Above=70, Avg=50, Below=10. None: Above=30, Avg=60, Below=60. Is there an association between participation and academic performance?

options.text: No, because all three groups have students at every performance level
Yes, because students with no activity have a much lower 'Above Average' rate (20%) compared to Sports (50%) and Clubs (53.8%)
No, because the totals for each group are different
Yes, but only for below-average students

quiz_questions.explanation: Sports Above Avg: 60/120 = 50%. Clubs Above Avg: 70/130 = 53.8%. None Above Avg: 30/150 = 20%. The large differences in conditional distributions show a clear association between participation and academic performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food company tests two packaging designs (Design A, Design B) across three store types (Supermarket, Convenience, Online). Supermarket: A=200 sold, B=180 sold. Convenience: A=80, B=120. Online: A=150, B=170. The company says Design A performs better overall. Is this correct, and is the evidence of association strong?

options.text: Yes; Design A sold 430 vs. 470 for B overall, but A only outperforms in supermarkets while B outperforms in convenience and online, showing an association between design and store type
Yes; Design A is universally better
No; the designs performed identically
No; Design B is worse in all categories

quiz_questions.explanation: Design A total: 430. Design B total: 470. Actually Design B sold more overall. But Design A outsold B in supermarkets (200 vs 180) while Design B outsold A in convenience (120 vs 80) and online (170 vs 150). This shows an association between packaging design preference and store type.

quiz_questions.question_text: A national survey of 2000 people records political affiliation (Party X, Party Y, Independent) and opinion on an issue (Support, Oppose). Party X: Support=450, Oppose=150. Party Y: Support=200, Oppose=500. Independent: Support=350, Oppose=350. A journalist writes 'Political affiliation is strongly associated with opinion on this issue.' Evaluate this claim.

options.text: The claim is not supported because some people in each party support the issue
The claim is supported: Party X supports at 75%, Party Y at 28.6%, and Independents at 50%, showing very different conditional distributions
The claim is wrong because the grand total is too large
The claim is wrong because correlation equals causation

quiz_questions.explanation: Party X: 450/600 = 75% support. Party Y: 200/700 = 28.6% support. Independent: 350/700 = 50% support. The large differences in conditional support rates (75% vs 28.6% vs 50%) strongly support the journalist's claim of association.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital studies the relationship between blood type (A, B, O, AB) and a certain condition (Has Condition, No Condition). Type A: Has=50, No=200. Type B: Has=30, No=120. Type O: Has=70, No=280. Type AB: Has=15, No=60. Do the data suggest an association between blood type and the condition?

options.text: Yes, because Type O has the most cases
No, because all blood types have approximately the same conditional rate of the condition (20%)
Yes, because the marginal frequencies differ
No, because blood type is not categorical

quiz_questions.explanation: Type A: 50/250 = 20%. Type B: 30/150 = 20%. Type O: 70/350 = 20%. Type AB: 15/75 = 20%. All blood types have the same 20% rate of the condition, indicating no association (the variables appear independent).

quiz_questions.question_text: A university examines the relationship between major (Science, Arts, Business) and employment within 6 months of graduation (Employed, Not Employed). Science: Employed=180, Not=20. Arts: Employed=100, Not=50. Business: Employed=160, Not=40. A parent argues that major choice does not affect employment. Does the data support this?

options.text: Yes, because most graduates in all majors are employed
No, because employment rates differ: Science 90%, Arts 66.7%, Business 80%, showing an association between major and employment
Yes, because all groups have employed graduates
No, because Business has the most graduates

quiz_questions.explanation: Science: 180/200 = 90%. Arts: 100/150 = 66.7%. Business: 160/200 = 80%. The different employment rates across majors show an association. The parent's claim is not supported by the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city surveys 1200 residents about transportation preference (Car, Bus, Bike) and neighborhood (Urban, Suburban, Rural). Urban: Car=80, Bus=150, Bike=70. Suburban: Car=200, Bus=100, Bike=50. Rural: Car=300, Bus=30, Bike=20. A city planner says 'There is a strong association between neighborhood and transportation choice.' Is the planner correct? What evidence supports this?

options.text: No, because cars are popular everywhere
Yes, because car usage rates differ dramatically: Urban 26.7%, Suburban 57.1%, Rural 85.7%, and bus/bike patterns also shift by neighborhood
No, because all neighborhoods use all transportation types
Yes, but only for bike users

quiz_questions.explanation: Urban car: 80/300 = 26.7%. Suburban car: 200/350 = 57.1%. Rural car: 300/350 = 85.7%. The conditional distributions change dramatically by neighborhood (Urban favors buses, Rural overwhelmingly favors cars), confirming a strong association.
